channel.title: Abstract-Algebra on Chen Kai Blog
channel.description: Recent content in Abstract-Algebra on Chen Kai Blog

item.title: Applications — Coding Theory, Cryptography, and Physics
item.description:
Algebra in the wild#

Five articles of definitions, theorems, proof sketches. Now the payoff. Abstract algebra is not a self-contained game — it’s the language behind some of the most important engineering and physics of the 20th and 21st centuries. Here are four concrete applications.

Error-correcting codes#

Digital communication is noisy. Bits flip. You need redundancy to detect and correct errors. The algebra of finite fields gives you that redundancy in an optimal way.

item.title: Galois Theory — Symmetry of Roots
item.description:
The big picture#

You have a polynomial. You want its roots. For quadratics, there’s the quadratic formula. Cubics and quartics have formulas too (ugly, but they exist). Quintics? Abel and Ruffini proved no general formula exists. Galois theory explains why — by translating the question into group theory.

The core idea: attach a group to a polynomial (its Galois group). The polynomial is solvable by radicals iff that group is a solvable group. The symmetric group $S_5$ is not solvable, and a generic quintic has Galois group $S_5$ . Done.

item.title: Fields and Field Extensions
item.description:
Fields: rings where everything divides#

A field is a commutative ring with unity in which every nonzero element has a multiplicative inverse. Equivalently, a field has exactly two ideals: $(0)$ and the whole ring.

$$ F \text{ is a field} \iff \forall\, a \neq 0,\; \exists\, a^{-1} : aa^{-1} = 1 $$

The standard examples: $\mathbb{Q}$ , $\mathbb{R}$ , $\mathbb{C}$ , and the finite fields $\mathbb{F}_p = \mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}$ for prime $p$ .

item.title: Rings, Ideals, and Polynomial Arithmetic
item.description:
From one operation to two#

Groups capture a single operation with invertibility. But most mathematical objects we work with daily — integers, polynomials, matrices — come with two operations: addition and multiplication. A ring is the framework that handles both.

The definition#

A ring $(R, +, \cdot)$ is a set with two operations such that:

item.title: Homomorphisms, Normal Subgroups, and Quotient Groups
item.description:
Maps that respect structure#

A function between groups that preserves the operation is called a homomorphism. This is the right notion of “map” in group theory — it tells you when two groups are structurally related, even if their elements look completely different.

The definition#

A group homomorphism $\varphi: G \to H$ satisfies:

$$ \varphi(ab) = \varphi(a)\varphi(b) \quad \forall\, a, b \in G $$

Consequences: $\varphi(e_G) = e_H$ and $\varphi(a^{-1}) = \varphi(a)^{-1}$ .

item.title: Groups and Subgroups — the Algebra of Symmetry
item.description:
Why groups?#

Rotate a square 90 degrees. It looks the same. Flip it, then rotate — still the same square. These operations compose, they associate, and each one can be undone. That pattern — composition, associativity, invertibility — shows up everywhere: clock arithmetic, matrix multiplication, permutations of a Rubik’s cube, gauge transformations in physics.

A group strips away everything except the composition rule and asks: what can we deduce from these three axioms alone?
